interactions.summary: Jiang finishes the Vietnam analogy by defining clear objectives, adaptation, and will to fight, then explains sunk cost through the casino image. He then moves to Ukraine, arguing that Ukraine should have traded space for overextension but instead fought for image, which lets him compare Zelenskyy's television logic to Trump.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang leading a lecture with intermittent classroom prompts; student responses are implied but mostly not transcribed.

speaker_notes.note: The passage contains rhetorical questions and one brief implied student answer; no separate speaker turns are available.

claims.claim: Jiang offers three questions for assessing who will win a war: whether the actor has clear military objectives, adapts to the battlefield, and retains the will to fight.
He argues that despite killing large numbers of Vietnamese, the United States did not destroy the enemy's will to fight and instead made more people willing to fight Americans.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical interpretation of the Vietnam War.

claims.claim: He says the United States stayed in Vietnam because of credibility, which he reinterprets as the sunk cost fallacy: having invested too much, leaders refuse to leave and admit the loss.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical model drawn from Vietnam and used as an Iran analog.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that the proper Ukrainian strategy against Russia in 2022 would have been to retreat, overextend Russian supply lines, encircle Russian forces, and disrupt their supply.
claims.temporal_scope: Retrospective assessment of the Russia-Ukraine war stated on 2024-05-29.

claims.claim: He claims Ukraine instead refused to give up land and later launched a counteroffensive into Russian artillery, which he presents as major mistakes that made Ukraine lose the war.
claims.temporal_scope: Assessment of Ukraine's war position as of 2024-05-29.

claims.claim: He says Ukraine's manpower crisis is so severe that the average age of its army is over 40 and men in their 60s and 70s are fighting.
claims.temporal_scope: Claim about Ukraine's military as of 2024-05-29.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that Zelenskyy's background and incentives make him care about what looks good on television rather than what works strategically.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive diagnosis of Zelenskyy stated on 2024-05-29.

claims.claim: He compares Trump to Zelenskyy as a leader who prioritizes television image and predicts Trump is likely to order a land invasion of Iran because it would look strong on TV.
claims.temporal_scope: Prediction made on 2024-05-29 about a possible second Trump term.

signature_moments.moment: War can be read through three questions: objectives, adaptation, and will to fight.
signature_moments.source_phrase: clear military objectives... adapt to the battlefield... the will to fight
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the episode a reusable analytic test for the historical analogs.

signature_moments.moment: War becomes a casino you cannot leave because you want the lost money back.
signature_moments.source_phrase: when you go in a casino, you start losing money. At some point, you cannot leave the casino
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The casino image carries Jiang's sunk cost model into the Iran scenario.

signature_moments.moment: The winning defense may be retreat: give space so the attacker outruns supply.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if you just give them space, they're going to outrun the supply lines
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It inverts ordinary political optics of defending every inch into a supply-line trap.

signature_moments.moment: Television image becomes the enemy of battlefield reality.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Reality, the reality on the ground is very different from TV
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This image connects Ukraine to Trump's likely Iran decision in Jiang's argument.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: One of Jiang's three war-outcome tests: whether an actor still has the will to continue fighting.
The refusal to leave a losing war because too much has already been invested and admitting loss would be humiliating.
A defensive strategy in which an attacker is allowed to move far enough that its supply lines can be encircled and disrupted.
The political production of an appearance of victory or strength that Jiang contrasts with battlefield reality.

chronology_notes.note: The Ukraine assessment is Jiang's position as of 2024-05-29 and should not be merged into later Ukraine or Iran-war claims without date-specific support.

uncertainty_notes.note: Claims that Ukraine has lost the war and about army age/manpower are Jiang's contested 2024 assessment; keep the source date attached.
